svg.text: Cache Stampede Prevention: Strategy Comparison and Decision Framework
Comparing Locking, Stale-While-Revalidate, Probabilistic Early Expiration, and Jittered TTLs for production-scale cache protection
When a popular cache key expires, hundreds of concurrent requests hit the origin simultaneously, causing a thundering herd that overwhelms the backend
Without Protection: Cache Key Expires → Thundering Herd
N concurrent identical queries | CPU 100% | Connection pool exhausted
Response time: 5-30s | Cascading failures across dependent services
Stampede Impact Metrics (Hot Key with 10K req/s)
- Guarantees exactly 1 origin query per expired key
- Strong consistency: all requesters get same fresh value
- Simple to implement with distributed locks (Redis SET NX)
- Works with any cache backend, no special features needed
- Waiting requests block for full origin fetch latency
- Lock contention under extreme concurrency adds overhead
- Lock holder failure (timeout/crash) leaves waiters stranded
- Increased tail latency for 99th percentile requests
How It Works: Serve stale + async refresh
- Zero additional latency for requesters (stale served instantly)
- Only 1 background refresh even under heavy concurrency
- No distributed lock needed; simpler coordination
- Serves stale data during SWR window (eventual consistency)
- Background thread may fail silently (stale persists)
- SWR window too short = repeated stampedes
- SWR window too long = excessively stale data
How It Works: Probability increases as TTL approaches expiry
If random() < P, this request triggers a refresh. Otherwise uses cached value.
At 80% TTL: P = 0.64, meaning ~64% of requests will refresh early
- Spreads refresh load across time (no synchronized stampede)
- Naturally scales with traffic: more requests = higher P
- Tunable via exponent (squaring, cubing) for different hotness
- Multiple requests may still refresh (not strictly single-flight)
- Requires careful tuning of probability function per workload
How It Works: Add random variance to TTL to desynchronize expiry
With 20% jitter on 300s TTL: actual expiry between 240s and 360s
- Simplest strategy: one-line change to TTL calculation
- No coordination, no locks, no background threads
- Prevents synchronized expiry across keys and instances
- Works with any cache system (client-side TTL manipulation)
- Doesn't prevent stampede, only reduces probability
- Extreme hot keys can still collide within jitter window
